hand.title: Daily Hand

events.text: The hand kicked off with a raise from your opponent, which you decided to call. The stage was set for a potentially explosive pot.
Flop — 6, 8, 9. Your opponent came out betting, and you chose to call, sensing a potential opportunity to strike back later.
Turn — Queen. The board didn't seem to improve your opponent's hand, yet they bet again — this time with a bit more aggression, aiming to protect their hand.
River — Queen. The final card sparked a large river bet from your opponent, indicating a hand that had recently improved and was now looking to maximize its value.

wrongReasons: 4 9 is close, but the river lead is the tell. The correct hand picked up more on the river than 4 9 would have.
6 6 makes a full house here — a hand that strong would have raised earlier and pushed harder for maximum value. The measured sizing in this hand points to something weaker.
3 7 has little reason to lead the river here — one pair can't comfortably go for value after calling two streets.
hand.explanation:
Answer: A 6

Your opponent's consistent betting pattern, especially the increased aggression on the turn, suggested a hand that was strong but not invincible. The fact that they only improved to Two Pair by the river, with the A and 6 making a pair with the board's Queen and 6, fits perfectly with the observed betting behavior, particularly the decision to bet all three streets with the sizing increasing as their hand improved on the river.
